one_line_takeaway: Proposes 'intent-to-execution integrity' -- the conjunction of Instruction, Data Flow, Judgment, and Tool Integrity -- as a unifying correctness target for LLM-agent security, and shows via a qualitative coverage table that no current defense satisfies all four.
key_finding: No existing defense achieves full coverage of all four integrity properties; coverage is partial and non-compositional. General-agent defenses (PromptArmor, PIA-Detection, TaskShield, Progent, CaMeL, DRIFT) protect the translation layer -- CaMeL is marked full (provable) on both Instruction Integrity and Data Flow Integrity, Progent full on Data Flow Integrity -- but all six leave Tool Integrity as none because they assume trusted tools, and nearly all give none/limited Judgment Integrity. OpenClaw/execution-layer defenses (NemoClaw, IronClaw, SeClaw, SafeClaw-R, SecureClaw) provide only partial Tool Integrity via sandboxing/auditing and mostly partial-or-none coverage of the other three. The central diagnosed gap is compositional: securing only the translation layer or only the execution layer is insufficient; a correctly selected tool call can still invoke a malicious skill, and a well-confined component can still be invoked for the wrong reason. Judgment Integrity is identified as the least-developed dimension and qualitatively hardest because the task requires reading the very untrusted data that can bias it.
core_contribution: A position paper arguing that securing LLM agents first requires a single end-to-end correctness property for the intent-to-execution pipeline (the process by which natural-language user intent is lowered into plans, tool calls, and system operations). Using an explicit analogy to compiler semantic preservation (CompCert), the authors identify two fundamental problem sources -- untrusted data ingestion and untrusted tool execution -- and derive four integrity properties that must hold simultaneously: Instruction Integrity, Data Flow Integrity, Judgment Integrity, and Tool Integrity. Their conjunction is named 'intent-to-execution integrity.' The paper's own novel contribution beyond prior formalization work (notably ref [13], Siu et al., which it explicitly positions against) is (a) dropping the trusted-tool assumption to add Tool Integrity for open third-party skill ecosystems, and (b) introducing Judgment Integrity to capture reasoning-level manipulation that produces no unauthorized tool call. It then applies this framework as an evaluation lens over 11 existing defense systems.
threat_model: Adversary corrupts the agent's intent-to-execution pipeline at one of three per-timestep operations: reasoning (untrusted data adversarially biases a semantic decision without any injected command, e.g. self-promotional text steering a paper-review agent), action construction (attribution confusion causing the wrong tool call, or provenance-violating data flowing into tool arguments, e.g. .env credentials exfiltrated in an outbound email), and execution (a third-party skill performing undeclared side effects such as reading an SSH key and exfiltrating it). Key emphasis is on supply-chain threats in open skill ecosystems (framed via a fictionalized 'OpenClaw'/'ClawHub' world) where tools are community-contributed and cannot be assumed trusted. The paper formalizes Judgment Integrity as J(q, d) = J(q, d + delta) for all task-irrelevant perturbations delta.
limitations: As a position paper it offers no empirical validation: the coverage ratings in Table 3 are the authors' qualitative judgments, not measured on any benchmark, and 'full (provable)' vs 'partial' is asserted rather than demonstrated. The sufficiency argument for the four properties is an informal case analysis, not a formal proof. Several evaluated OpenClaw systems are assessed from 'public design' descriptions rather than tested. The motivating ecosystem is presented under placeholder/anonymized names (OpenClaw, ClawHub, ClawHavoc, NemoClaw, etc.), which obscures verifiability of the concrete incident claims. The authors concede Judgment Integrity is hard to formalize and that no known technique can guarantee it, and that Tool Integrity behavioral conformance and Data Flow policy specification do not yet scale to large third-party ecosystems.

quantitative_results: Table 3 rates 11 defense systems on 4 properties with full/partial/none; CaMeL is rated full (provable) on Instruction Integrity and Data Flow Integrity; Progent full on Data Flow Integrity; all 6 general-agent defenses rated none on Tool Integrity; no system rated full on Judgment Integrity or across all four properties (authors' own qualitative assessment, not measured)
Cited (not the paper's own result): the ClawHavoc campaign [15] planted over 1,000 malicious skills to harvest credentials and cryptocurrency wallets
Cited (not the paper's own result): a large-scale audit of 42,447 skills [17] found 26.1% contain at least one security vulnerability
Cited (not the paper's own result): a report [24] of 341 malicious skills stealing data from agent users
baselines_compared: General-agent defenses analyzed (not experimentally compared): PromptArmor [20], PIA-Detection [16], TaskShield [12], Progent [25], CaMeL [19], DRIFT [14]
Execution-layer / open-ecosystem defenses analyzed: NemoClaw [2], IronClaw [3], SeClaw [5], SafeClaw-R [6], SecureClaw [11]
Positioned against prior agent-security formalization [13] (Siu et al.), which the paper argues assumes trusted tools and covers only the planning layer
